intro.value: Eq. 12 is the onset condition for the nonzero synchronized branch, and Eq. 13 is the first concrete choice of frequency distribution used to evaluate that condition explicitly.
So this transition has two parts: first identify the critical coupling K sub c from the general threshold equation, then choose a specific g of omega so that the threshold becomes an explicit number.

left.value: Equation 11 still contains both possibilities: the trivial incoherent state r equals 0, and a nonzero synchronized branch with r greater than 0.
Eq. 12 comes only from the second branch. We get it by dividing Eq. 11 by r, which is valid only when r is nonzero.
So the statement K less than K sub c implies r equals 0 is not read directly off Eq. 12 by itself. It comes from comparing the trivial incoherent branch with the nonzero branch that Eq. 12 describes.
The nonzero synchronized branch bifurcates from r equals 0, so to find the onset threshold we take the limit as r goes to 0 from the positive side in Eq. 12.
At r equals 0, the argument of g becomes 0 everywhere, so only g of 0 survives. That is why the threshold depends on g of 0 rather than the whole shape of the distribution.

Physically, g of 0 is the density of oscillators near zero detuning in the rotating frame. If many oscillators already sit near that center, they are easier to lock, so the threshold K sub c is smaller.

At that point we do evaluate the remaining integral, but only after taking the onset limit. We are not solving Eq. 12 for arbitrary r here. We first replace g of K r sine theta by g of 0, then integrate cosine squared theta from minus pi over 2 to pi over 2.
When K is greater than K sub c, coupling is strong enough to create a coherent locked group. The phase vectors no longer cancel completely, so the order parameter becomes positive.
This does not mean every oscillator is identical or locked. It means enough of the population forms an aligned cluster that the average phase vector has nonzero length.
So K sub c is the minimum coupling needed for macroscopic order to appear.

right.latex: r = 0 \text{ means incoherence}, \qquad r > 0 \text{ means partial synchronization}

left.value: Eq. 12 is still general because it only tells us that the threshold depends on g of 0. To go further, we must choose an explicit frequency distribution.
Eq. 13 makes that choice: it takes g of omega to be a Lorentzian. Then g of 0 becomes 1 over pi gamma, so the threshold becomes K sub c equals 2 gamma.
So Eq. 13 is not derived from Eq. 12. It is substituted into Eq. 12.
